title: Leaving Comments 📝
description: Learn how to document your code so humans can read it.

slides.title: Notes for Humans 🧠
slides.content:
# Why Comment?

Code is for computers to read. Comments are for *humans* to read.

A comment is a way to leave a note in your code that Ruby will completely ignore when running the program.

Use comments to explain **why** a piece of code works or what it's supposed to do.

slides.title: The Hash Symbol #️⃣
slides.content:
To write a comment in Ruby, use the `#` symbol (hashtag or pound sign).

Everything on a line after the `#` will be ignored by Ruby.

```ruby
# This is a comment!
puts "This runs just fine."
```

slides.title: Comments in Action
slides.content: Notice how only the `puts` lines display text, while the comments are totally silent.
slides.codeSnippet:
# I can write whatever I want here.
puts "You can see me!"

puts "This works too!" # I'm an inline comment!

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: Which symbol is used for comments?

quizData.question: Which symbol designates a single-line comment in Ruby?
quizData.explanation: Ruby uses the hash `#` symbol. Anything on the same line after it gets ignored.

slides.title: Multi-line Comments 📚
slides.content:
If you need a really long comment, you can use multiple `#` symbols on each line.

```ruby
# This is a really long
# comment that spans
# multiple lines.
```

You can also use `=begin` and `=end` to create a multi-line comment block, but convention usually prefers multiple `#`s.

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: What happens to the code following a `#` on the same line?

quizData.question: If you add a comment at the very end of a line of code, what does Ruby do with it?

options.text: It reads the code, and ignores everything after the # symbol.

quizData.explanation: This is known as an 'inline comment' and Ruby simply ignores the comment part while running the code before the `#`.
